label: Lie Group
definition: A group that is also a smooth manifold, so its symmetry operations form a continuum you can differentiate, the mathematics of continuous symmetry like rotation. Its decisive trick is that the curved group is governed near the identity by a flat linear object, its Lie algebra, which is what makes continuous symmetry computable and lets a model predict an unconstrained generator and exponentiate it into a valid rotation.

body.text: A Lie group, named for Sophus Lie, is at once a group and a smooth manifold, with multiplication and inversion being smooth maps. It is the right notion when the symmetries of a problem form a continuum rather than a finite list. The rotations of the plane, parameterized by one angle, form a circle; the rotations of three-dimensional space form a curved three-dimensional manifold; the invertible matrices of a given size, the translations of space, and the rigid motions that combine rotation and translation are all Lie groups. Because the elements vary smoothly, you can do calculus on the symmetry itself: differentiate a family of transformations, take limits of small ones, and move continuously from the identity to any nearby element.

The decisive simplification is that a Lie group, however curved, is governed near its identity by a flat linear object, its Lie algebra, the tangent space at the identity with a bracket operation. The Lie algebra captures infinitesimal symmetries, the velocities of one-parameter families of transformations through the identity, and the exponential map sends these generators back to actual group elements, wrapping the flat algebra onto the curved group. So a hard nonlinear question about the group can often be answered by a tractable linear question about its algebra, then exponentiated, which is the reason continuous symmetry is computationally manageable at all and ties group theory to differential geometry inseparably.

Lie groups reach machine learning through equivariance. When a task is symmetric under a continuous group, such as the rotation group for many vision and molecular problems, building a model equivariant to that group by construction is far more data-efficient than learning the symmetry from examples. The discrete translation equivariance that makes a convolutional neural network effective generalizes, through Lie-group theory, to networks equivariant to continuous rotations and to the full group of rigid motions, so a learned feature responds identically no matter how its input is rotated, exactly the inductive bias physical and geometric data demand.

The Lie algebra also gives a clean way to represent and optimize over group elements. A rotation is awkward to parameterize directly without redundancy or singularities, but its generator in the Lie algebra is an unconstrained linear quantity, so a model predicts the small generator and exponentiates to obtain a guaranteed-valid rotation; the same trick lets gradient-based optimization move along the group by stepping in the flat algebra and mapping onto the manifold, which underlies pose estimation and Riemannian optimization over matrix groups. More broadly, decomposing how a group acts on a signal into irreducible representations is the harmonic analysis behind generalizations of the Fourier transform to spheres and other symmetric spaces, powering spherical convolutions and steerable filters. The throughline is that a Lie group lets you treat a continuum of symmetries with calculus and linear algebra at once, one of the most principled inductive biases in modern machine learning.

image.alt: A unit circle in the plane with the point 1 marked on the right at the origin's level, and three points z, w, and zw on the upper arc.
image.caption: The circle is the simplest Lie group: a group that is also a smooth manifold. Its points are unit-length complex numbers, the group operation multiplies them (which adds their angles, so z times w lands at zw), the identity is the point 1, and every element has an inverse. Because the points vary smoothly, you can do calculus on the symmetry itself, which is what makes continuous symmetry tractable.
